channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Can Three Auction Houses Sell $2.6 Billion Worth of Art in One Week?
item.description: Five luxury artworks hold the key to the spring season, one of the most anticipated sales in years. Major buyers are looking past female and younger artists and toward tradition.
item.dc:creator: Tim F. Schneider, Julia Halperin and Zachary Small

item.title: How Much Art Is Too Much? A Guide to the New York Fairs.
item.description: Latin American galleries. World-class photography. Upcycled design. Art fairs will take over New York this week. Here are seven (of many) worth a visit.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman

item.title: If You See Only 6 Venice Shows, Make It These
item.description: Thought-provoking and senses-stirring works on war, art patronage, and the Black experience in America are just some of our critics’ picks.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago, Blake Gopnik and Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ashfika Rahman’s “Than Para — No Land Without Us” (2025-ongoing) in the “Still Joy” group exhibition at the Pinchuk Art Center.

item.title: Rocky Has Entered the Building
item.description: The statue has long drawn fans to the steps of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, but now it’s inside, anchoring an exhibition that investigates race, activism and violence.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Hawthorne

item.title: In This Nazi-Era Restitution Dispute, the Focus Turns to a Missing Cow
item.description: A family is vying for the return of a painting it thought was by Rubens. But an expert says it’s a copy because it does not include the artist’s depiction of a peeing cow.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Hickley

item.title: Matthew Wong’s Grieving Mother Protects His Artistic Legacy
item.description: The troubled painter Matthew Wong’s star was on the rise when he died at 35. His mother, Monita Wong, is making sure his work is seen, especially in Venice.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow
item.media:credit: Alana Paterson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Monita Wong holds a mask that her son, the artist Matthew Wong, who died by suicide in 2019, made in the fourth grade in Hong Kong.

item.title: Paul Klee’s ‘Angelus Novus’ Joins Show at Jewish Museum in New York
item.description: “Angelus Novus,” an artwork with a fascinating back story, including most recently a wartime delay, is a late addition to an already impressive Klee survey at the Jewish Museum.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Solomon

item.title: A Fashion Revolution at the Met
item.description: With “Costume Art,” the dress department moves out of the basement to become the gateway to the museum.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman and Marcus Maddox
item.media:description: Unexpected bedfellows: The entry room of “Costume Art,” focusing on the naked and nude body, sets the tone for the show, with every garment paired with an accompanying piece from around the museum.

item.title: Venice Biennale: Austria, Japan, Belgium and Other Big Hits
item.description: A doll-focused show, a contemplative garden, a sizzling hothouse: It’s all to see at the world’s top contemporary art event.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago, Alex Marshall, Laura Rysman, Aruna D’Souza and Julia Halperin
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the pavilion, performers scurry up and down wooden racks that hold plaster tablets with words in various languages embossed on them.

item.title: At the Venice Biennale, an American Artist Plops His Sculptures on a World Stage
item.description: They may be bronze and marble, but Alma Allen’s artworks at the Venice Biennale are as intangible as air.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alma Allen on an international stage in Venice. Right, “Not Yet Titled” (2014), yule marble. Left, “Not Yet Titled” (2020), bronze, at the U.S. Pavilion.

item.title: Artists’ Strike Closes Pavilions at Venice Biennale, Adding to Upheaval
item.description: The hottest exhibitions at the world’s major art exhibition were shuttered on Friday as part of a pro-Palestinian demonstration.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Netherlands’ shuttered Venice Biennale pavilion displayed a flag and fliers in support of Palestinians on Friday.

item.title: Without Its Expert Jury, Venice Biennale Lets the Public Choose Its Prize Winners
item.description: The jury resigned last week, so the international art event is letting viewers pick the best artist from the main exhibition and the national pavilions.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd of visitors at the Giardini in Venice during the Biennale preview days on Thursday.

item.title: How Florentina Holzinger Made Austria’s Pavilion the Venice Biennale’s Big Hit
item.description: Undoubtedly the biggest talker at this year’s Venice Biennale is the Austrian pavilion, where visitors can make their own contributions to the work on show.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Holzinger and her team say there is something profound beneath the “Seaworld Venice” bombast...

item.title: Is It an Art Gallery? A Museum? A Theater? A Dream?
item.description: The Ministry of Awe, a new immersive experience in a former bank in Philadelphia, aims to help locate the wondrous in the everyday.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Michelle Gustafson
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the exhibit “Heavens,” Imani Lee Williams plays the role of High Priestess.

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in May
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Will Heinrich covers Ceija Stojka’s naïve expressionism, Mao Ishikawa’s sprawling bodies and a reinstallation of an important show by Peter Hujar.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich

item.title: Drawings Dominate at a Chicago de Kooning Exhibit
item.description: In an exception to the usual paintings-heavy blockbuster exhibitions, the Art Institute of Chicago is presenting the masterful drawings of Willem de Kooning.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: In Tuscany, an Artistic Sculpture Hub Thrives
item.description: Pietrasanta, in the Tuscan region of Italy, has become an international sculpture hub that bursts with activity, especially in summer.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri
item.media:credit: Emanuele Camerini for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Armenian-born artist Mikayel Ohanjanyan at his studio. Seven of his sculptures are currently on display in Pietrasanta.

item.title: A Photographer of Newark’s People Gets a Show Among the People
item.description: A number of works by Manuel Acevedo are being displayed throughout the city in outdoor spots highly visible to the residents who inspired them.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.category: Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey

item.title: The Swiss Collector Building a Massive Trove of Chinese Art
item.description: Over three decades, Sigg, a Swiss businessman and former diplomat, amassed thousands of contemporary Chinese works. Ai Weiwei calls him “my maker.”
item.dc:creator: Mercedes Hutton
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Swiss art collector Uli Sigg at his home, near Lucerne, Switzerland. He amassed a huge trove of contemporary Chinese art, much of which he has donated to M+ museum in Hong Kong.

item.title: Spotlighting the Woman Who Brought European Modernism to California
item.description: Although Galka Scheyer might be an unfamiliar name, the artists she championed have become famous. An exhibition in Pasadena brings her to the foreground.
item.dc:creator: Will Higginbotham

item.title: How to See Rare Books in London
item.description: Here’s a guide to illuminated manuscripts, antique tomes and first editions around the British capital.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The British Library’s collection of more than 170 million items includes more than 13.5 million printed books and e-books.

item.title: Leigh Magar, High-End Milliner Turned Indigo Artist, Dies at 57
item.description: She was known for bespoke hats that attracted a celebrity following. Then she left Charleston for an island off South Carolina, where she began cultivating indigo.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: Candace Dane Chambers for The New York Times

item.title: In Cleveland, Smokers Are Helping to Keep the Arts Alive
item.description: A novel cigarette tax has generated $270 million for cultural organizations in the Cleveland area, which makes declining smoking rates “a double-edged sword.”
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ross Mantle
item.category: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and Museum
item.media:description: Playhouse Square, in downtown Cleveland, has used grants from a cigarette tax to explore creating a designated theater district akin to Times Square or the West End.

item.title: A Taxidermist Gives Dead Animals a New Life
item.description: The creation, care and keeping of creatures is a responsibility the last full-time museum taxidermist in the U.S. takes both seriously and joyfully.
item.dc:creator: Jordan Kisner and Sasha Arutyunova
item.category: Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County

item.title: At Frieze New York and Beyond, Indigenous Artists Are in the Spotlight
item.description: With works showing in New York, Venice and elsewhere, Sara Flores, Suzanne Kite and Seba Calfuqueo are exploding past notions of what, exactly, Indigenous art is.
item.dc:creator: Nazanin Lankarani

item.title: Maya Lin Connects Nature to a New Manhattan Skyscraper and Beyond
item.description: The famed artist and designer drew on her lifelong love of the environment for inspiration for new work in New York and Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Jane L. Levere
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lin’s new work at the JPMorgan Chase building in New York, a sculpture composed on massive gray stone walls, and the adjoining plaza were envisioned in part as a respite from the cacophony of the city.

item.title: New Books Provide Divergent Views of the Art Market
item.description: Three new books — a sweeping work of nonfiction, a cheeky memoir and a dual biography — provide divergent views on the business of buying and selling, and they are out just in time for New York Art Week.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: Michael Joo Steps Into the Art World’s Spotlight
item.description: Michael Joo is appearing in multiple shows, from New York and the San Francisco Bay Area to a Mediterranean island and Venice.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times

item.title: A Panorama of Design during the NYCxDesign Festival
item.description: A look at news, products and events during the NYCxDesign Festival.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Joseph Kramm
item.media:description: Chain mail lighting and a tapestry by Loney Abrams and Johnny Stanish, the husband-and-wife duo and founders of Wretched Flowers.

item.title: A Monument to Chocolate Is Wrapped in Layers of Mexican History
item.description: Combining a colonial house and a contemporary addition, the new Museum of Cacao & Chocolate sits on a grisly Aztec souvenir.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Gallow
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Huei, or great Tzompantli. It displays hundreds of human skulls belonging to people who were believed to have been sacrificed in the 15th century.

item.title: Before SoHo, This Building Was at the Heart of New York’s Arts District
item.description: A West Side co-op built for artists and later turned into offices is being reborn as a luxury condominium named Parc Beaufort.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies
item.media:credit: Stefano Ukmar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The model one-bedroom apartment at the Parc Beaufort development on West 57th Street.

item.title: These Artisans, Showing at TEFAF New York, Push the Limits of Materials
item.description: A couple grow their own furniture. Another artist polishes metal to a mirror sheen. A third collaborates with insects. Together, they are redefining craft.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa

item.title: Dynamic Artistic Duos are on Display in New York This Spring
item.description: Three exhibitions are pairing artists to reveal how their works complement — or contrast with — each other.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: The Korean Artist Park Daesung Is In the Spotlight at 81
item.description: The Korean artist is nearly 81, but his works have been featured in shows in Chicago, London, Washington and Los Angeles. Now they are also headed to TEFAF New York.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Park Daesung at his studio in Gyeongju, South Korea. Gana Art, a major gallery in Seoul, will take four of his paintings to TEFAF New York.

item.title: On the Hunt for a Memphis Group-Style Lamp
item.description: On the hunt for a light that only looked like it came from the Memphis Group.
item.dc:creator: Tom Delavan
item.media:credit: via Instagram @mondoblogo; Getty Images

item.title: Why Is Everyone Obsessed With Bogs?
item.description: From fashion to art, an explainer on our love of wetlands.
item.dc:creator: Zoey Poll

item.title: A Stroll Through the Mansions of New York’s Gilded Age
item.description: Yes, some memorable mansions were lost to the vagaries of expenses and development, but many remain — and some are even open to the public. A walking guide.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Andrew Carnegie residence on 91st Street is now the Cooper Hewitt, Smithsonian Design Museum.

item.title: Dealers Face a Choice: Show Solo Artists? Or Groups?
item.description: The decision can vary from fair to fair but sometimes solo booths can help visitors focus their attention on a single artist, rather than being overwhelmed by the art of many.
item.dc:creator: Annabel Keenan

item.title: From France to the U.S., an Exhibition on the Luxury Ties That Bind
item.description: Dozens of French luxury houses and institutions will converge on Manhattan for the cross-cultural exhibition “Hidden Treasures: 250 Years of Franco-American Luxury Stories.”
item.dc:creator: Tina Isaac-Goizé

item.title: Our Favorite Milan Homes
item.description: For centuries, the city has been a capital of design. Here, we revisit the most beautiful and exciting interiors we’ve covered.
item.dc:creator: T Magazine

item.title: Art Abounds on Campuses Outside of New York City
item.description: Academic museums at Princeton, Yale, Cornell and Skidmore have organized several standout exhibitions, worth a venture beyond TEFAF New York.
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets
item.category: Johnson, Herbert F, Museum of Art
Tang Teaching Museum and Art Gallery

item.title: Uncertainty, But Also Optimism, Mark New York Art Week
item.description: This May, big-ticket items, and numerous major private collections, will be on offer and provide a major test of the market in a chaotic time.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times
item.media:description: TEFAF expects to host some 20,000 visitors at the Park Avenue Armory for TEFAF New York, with about 90 booths presenting contemporary and modern art.

item.title: In Cartagena, a Place for Monks and Movie Lovers Is Now a Hotel
item.description: In Cartagena, Colombia, historic buildings, including a monastery and a nightclub, were transformed into a Four Seasons.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi

item.title: This Film Provides a Trapezoidal Window into Former Pizza Huts
item.description: A documentary about the popular chain presents its repurposed buildings as protective shells where new businesses can grow.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times
item.media:description: The filmmakers Matthew Salleh and Rose Tucker at a Pizza Hut in Newburgh, N.Y. Their documentary “Slice of Life: The American Dream. In Former Pizza Huts.” came out last year.

item.title: France Passes Law Easing Process of Returning Looted Art
item.description: President Emmanuel Macron has long called the restitution of African art from French museums a priority. Experts say the new law is a seismic moment in that effort.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Corentin Fohlen for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Quai Branly museum in Paris. For decades, when countries demanded restitution, they were rebuffed by a French law deeming everything in the public collection “inalienable,” meaning that it cannot leave.

item.title: Review: Sophie Rivera’s Photos Come Out From the Shadows
item.description: “Double Exposures,” the first museum survey for the artist, highlights a career spent documenting New Yorkers, in ways both traditional and strange.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: It’s Time to Upgrade Your Cabinet Pulls
item.description: Plus: a Bruges bed-and-breakfast with a wild garden, artworks made on soaked canvases and more recommendations from T Magazine.

item.title: Inside the Artist Peter Bradley’s Upstate New York Home
item.description: Peter Bradley, a painter, art dealer and fixture of the 1960s and ’70s Manhattan art scene, brought a loftlike aesthetic to his lovingly restored 18th-century home.
item.dc:creator: Kate Guadagnino
